ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for fruit cake; A00NG (Cakes and pastries) is the closest defensible broader category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for fruit cake as a food item; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of fruit cake is one slice of approximately 80 g, as commonly portioned for dessert or festive occasions.

nutrient_source.title: Fruit cake — Singapore Health Promotion Board Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record carries HPB provenance at confidence 0.95 for all per_100g fields. Values are accepted as-is from the HPB Foodo database. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for fruitcake (NDB 18139) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat; this adaptation is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 54 (low) is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for fruit cake (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). The input record cited GI 55 (medium) from an 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source. The approved University of Sydney GI Database reports GI 54 for fruit cake, classified as low (GI <55). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (54 × 43.0) / 100 = 23.2, using 80 g serving with 53.6 g/100 g carbohydrate = 42.9 g available carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (360 mg/100 g) combined with very high sugar (34.9 g/100 g); excess sugar intake independently elevates blood pressure via insulin-mediated sodium retention and sympathetic nervous system activation.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit fruit cake to an occasional very small portion (30–40 g) and avoid alcohol-soaked varieties, as sodium at 360 mg/100 g and sugar at 34.9 g/100 g together present an unfavourable cardiovascular load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high sugar content (34.9 g/100 g) and high total carbohydrate (53.6 g/100 g) with only moderate dietary fibre (3.3 g/100 g) produce a high glycaemic load per serving (GL ~23), significantly elevating postprandial blood glucose.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid or strictly limit fruit cake; a single 80 g slice delivers approximately 43 g carbohydrate and 28 g sugar, causing a substantial postprandial glucose spike that is difficult to manage with standard medication regimens.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at approximately 5.8 g/100 g approaches the 6 g/100 g CVD threshold, and very high sugar (34.9 g/100 g) contributes to dyslipidaemia, elevated triglycerides, and endothelial inflammation.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat fruit cake as an occasional indulgence only, keeping portions to 40–50 g maximum, as the combination of saturated fat, high sugar, and moderate sodium collectively elevates LDL cholesterol, triglycerides, and blood pressure risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sugar at 34.9 g/100 g exceeds the 15 g/100 g RA refined-sugar threshold, promoting pro-inflammatory cytokine release; saturated fat at ~5.8 g/100 g also exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or strictly limit fruit cake due to its high refined sugar and saturated fat content, both of which promote systemic inflammation and may exacerbate joint symptoms; alcohol-soaked versions are additionally contraindicated with RA medications.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fruit cake is a dense, moist baked cake made with a mixture of candied or dried fruits (such as raisins, sultanas, currants, glacé cherries, and mixed peel), chopped nuts, and warming spices (such as cinnamon, nutmeg, and mixed spice), bound together with a butter-based batter of flour, eggs, and sugar. The cake is baked slowly at low temperature to prevent the fruit from burning and to allow the dense crumb to set fully. Traditional British and Commonwealth versions are often soaked or 'fed' with brandy, rum, or sherry over weeks or months, which preserves the cake and deepens its flavour. The texture is characteristically dense, sticky, and chewy, with a rich, sweet, spiced flavour profile and a dark brown crumb from the caramelised sugars and fruit. Fruit cake is strongly associated with festive occasions including Christmas, weddings, and celebrations across the United Kingdom, Australia, Singapore, and other Commonwealth countries. Nutritionally, fruit cake is energy-dense at 359 kcal/100 g, with high total carbohydrate (53.6 g/100 g) and very high sugar (34.9 g/100 g) from the concentrated dried and candied fruits. Fat content is moderate at 13.9 g/100 g, primarily from butter. Sodium is elevated at 360 mg/100 g. Dietary fibre is 3.3 g/100 g, contributed by the dried fruit and nuts. The glycaemic index is approximately 54 (low-medium boundary), but the glycaemic load per standard serving (80 g) is high at approximately 23, making it clinically significant for blood glucose management. Fruit cake is not suitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease except as a very occasional small portion.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat and micronutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central (NDB 18139, fruitcake) as HPB does not separately report these fields; this adaptation is documented. GI value is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. (2008). Overall confidence is high given the combination of two approved primary sources.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (5.8 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (5.3 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.6 g/100 g), trans fat (0.1 g/100 g), cholesterol (55 mg/100 g), potassium (233 mg/100 g), vitamin C (0.4 mg/100 g), vitamin A (28 mcg RAE/100 g), thiamine (0.09 mg/100 g), riboflavin (0.08 mg/100 g), niacin (0.9 mg/100 g), vitamin B6 (0.06 mg/100 g), vitamin B12 (0.1 mcg/100 g), folate (14 mcg DFE/100 g), vitamin E (0.5 mg/100 g), phosphorus (90 mg/100 g), magnesium (18 mg/100 g), zinc (0.5 mg/100 g), and water (22.0 g/100 g) are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for fruitcake (NDB 18139), as HPB Foodo does not separately report these fields. 2. vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are set to -1: detailed vitamin D and selenium data for fruit cake are not available in either the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 18139) at the required precision. 3. GI value updated from input record's 54 (low) per University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008); the input cited GI 55 from an unapproved 'Embedded GI Database'. 4. The dietary_tag 'plant_based' from the input has been removed as fruit cake contains eggs, butter, and dairy and is not plant-based. 5. No zero-value core macros are present; zero-macro justification rule is not triggered. 6. Halal flag set to true for standard non-alcohol-soaked versions; alcohol-soaked variants would not be halal.
